title: Privacy Coins Are Exploding...Despite Government Crackdowns
transcript: In  2025,  privacy  coins  got  hit  hard  by   regulators.  Monero  alone  got  delisted   from  73  exchanges  while  countries  like   India  ordered  exchanges  to  delist   privacy  coins  entirely.  But  somehow  they   were  also  one  of  the  best  performing   sectors  [music]  in  crypto  that  year.   Zcash  rallied  over  800%.  Monero  tripled   despite  getting  kicked  off  most  major   platforms.  So,  is  the  market  just   chasing  whatever  is  hot  or  something   deeper  going  on?  Something  about  what   kind  of  financial  system  people  actually   want?   A  lot  of  it  traces  back  to  a  single   tweet.  On  October  1st,  2025,  Naval   Ravocant,  founder  of  Angel  List,  one  of   the  most  followed  voices  in  Silicon   Valley,  tweeted,  "Bitcoin  is  insurance   against  fiat.  Zcash  is  insurance  against   Bitcoin.  [music]  Zcash  jumped  27%  within   hours  and  by  mid-  November  it  was   touching  $740.   Now  Naval  isn't  exactly  a  neutral   observer  here.  He  invested  $715,000   in  the  company  that  developed  Zcash  back   in  [music]  2015.  So  he  has  a  direct   financial  stake  in  this  and  critics   pointed  that  [music]  out  immediately.   And  if  you  want  to  be  technical  about   it,  the  insurance  framing  doesn't  really   hold  up.  [music]  Insurance  hedges   against  something  by  moving  in  the   opposite  direction.  And  Zcash  just  moves   with  Bitcoin.  But  none  of  that  stopped   the  rally,  which  raises  the  question  of   why  it  resonated  so  strongly  in  the   first  place.  One  possibility  is  timing.   By  October  2025,  the  surveillance   infrastructure  around  crypto  was   building  fast.  The  EU's  DAC  8  directive   was  set  to  go  live  in  January  this  year,   which  would  force  exchanges  to   automatically  report  your  holdings  to   tax  authorities.  CBDC's  were  in  active   pilots  across  China,  India,  and  Europe,   all  built  with  full  transaction   traceability.  The  Financial  Action  Task   Force  had  pushed  85  countries  to  pass   travel  rule  legislation  requiring   exchanges  to  collect  and  share  customer   info  on  every  transaction.  And  when  you   look  at  what  people  were  actually  doing   with  Zcash,  by  late  2025,  about  30%  of   the  supply  had  moved  into  shielded   addresses,  the  ones  that  encrypt   everything.  And  that  was  only  around  5%   2  years  earlier.  So  maybe  Naval's  tweet   didn't  create  the  demand.  It  just  put   words  to  what  was  already  happening.   What's  also  interesting  is  that  all   those  D-listings  and  bans  that  we   mentioned  didn't  hit  every  privacy  coin   equally.  In  places  like  India  and  South   Korea,  privacy  coins  got  banned  across   the  board.  But  where  regulators  leave   room  for  discretion,  Zcash  tends  to   survive  while  Monero  gets  dropped.  ZEC   is  still  trading  on  major  platforms  and   the  difference  comes  down  to  how  they're   built.  Monero  uses  mandatory  privacy,   meaning  every  single  transaction  is   encrypted,  no  exceptions.  Zcash  gives   you  a  choice.  You  can  use  transparent   addresses  that  work  like  Bitcoin,  fully   visible  on  the  blockchain,  or  you  can   use  shielded  addresses  that  encrypt   everything.  And  privacy  advocates  have  a   problem  with  this.  They  argue  that  if   only  some  people  use  the  private  option,   those  people  stand  out.  But  to   regulators  and  exchanges,  that   difference  matters.  And  so  Zcash  gets   more  room  to  operate.  Now  the  irony  is   that  while  regulators  are  cutting   privacy  coins  off  from  traditional   finance,  they're  also  building  the   strongest  argument  for  why  people  might   want  them.  137  countries  are  now   exploring  or  developing  central  bank   digital  currencies.  China's  digital  yuan   is  live  across  30  plus  cities.  India's  e   rupee  hit  over  10  billion  rupees  in   circulation  by  March  2025.  Europe  is   targeting  a  digital  euro  decision  by   2026  and  the  pattern  is  consistent   across  all  of  them.  Money  that  can  be   tracked  and  controlled  in  ways  physical   cash  never  could.  For  example,  China's   CBDC  has  programmable  features  such  as   spending  restrictions  and  expiration   dates.  In  one  Shenzen  trial,  they   programmed  vouchers  to  expire  and  90%   got  spent  before  the  deadline.  Now   combine  that  with  the  automatic   reporting  that  we  mentioned  earlier.   Exchanges  reporting  your  holdings  to  tax   authorities  and  the  surveillance  isn't   theoretical  anymore.  People  can  actually   see  what  the  future  of  money  looks  like.   Fully  traceable,  reportable,  and   programmable.  And  for  some,  that's   exactly  what  they  want.  Better  fraud   prevention,  cleaner  tax  enforcement,   more  efficient  policy.  And  for  others,   it  is  a  reason  to  look  for  alternatives.   And  when  they  do,  privacy  coins  are   what's  there.  And  it's  not  just  retail   users  looking  for  these  alternatives.   The  same  privacy  problems  show  up  at  the   institutional  level  as  well,  just  for   very  different  reasons.  If  you're  a   hedge  fund  executing  a  large  trade  on  a   public  blockchain,  everyone  can  see  your   position.  Other  traders  can  frontr  run   you  before  you  finish  executing.  If   you're  a  corporation  using  blockchain   for  supply  chain  or  crossber  payments,   your  competitors  can  see  your   transaction  volumes,  suppliers,  [music]   and  cash  flow  patterns.  Protocols  like   rail  gun  and  secret  network  let  you   transact  on  public  blockchains  while   keeping  the  details  private  using  zero   knowledge  proofs  to  verify  a  transaction   is  valid  without  revealing  what  it  is.   Now,  they  aren't  mainstream  or  widely   adopted  yet,  but  even  Vitalic  Bdderin   has  used  rail  gun  in  June  last  year  to   move  over  $2.6  million.  Now,  the   question  is  whether  privacy  coins  can   actually  survive  real  threats.  So,  we   talked  about  the  D-listings  and  bans,   but  what  if  this  goes  fully  global?  By   2025,  97  countries  had  updated  their   privacy  coin  regulations.  And  that  is  up   from  79  just  2  years  earlier.  But,  you   know,  it's  still  a  patchwork.  The  real   threat  is  a  global  consensus  that   privacy  coins  can't  coexist  with   financial  oversight.  Now,  is  that   likely?  Probably  not.  Getting  every   major  economy  to  agree  on  anything  is   hard.  Countries  have  different   priorities,  political  systems,  and   relationships  with  crypto.  But  if  it   happens,  the  infrastructure  will   probably  collapse  with  no  easy  way  to   convert  the  privacy  coins  [music]  to   dollars.  peer-to-peer  platforms  saw  a   19%  jump  in  activity  after  the   D-listings  last  year,  and  that's  where   people  would  most  likely  go.  But  P2P   can't  replace  the  level  of  scale  and   ease  offered  by  centralized  exchanges.   So,  even  if  privacy  coins  wouldn't  die   in  such  a  scenario,  they  would  just  get   pushed  so  far  to  the  margins  that  for   most  people,  they  would  just  be   impossible  to  access.  Now,  the  second   threat  is  quantum  computing,  and  the   timeline  just  got  a  lot  shorter.  In   November  last  year,  Vitalic  Bdderin   warned  that  quantum  computers  could   break  the  cryptography  securing  Bitcoin,   Ethereum,  and  privacy  coins  as  early  as   2028.  He  cited  prediction  markets  that   put  the  probability  at  20%  by  2030.  For   privacy  coins,  the  risk  is  double.  A   quantum  computer  could  not  only  break   encryption  to  steal  funds,  it  could  also   decrypt  years  of  past  transactions,   completely  undermining  the  privacy  that   these  coins  were  built  to  protect.  Zcash   says  it  has  actually  been  preparing  for   this  for  years.  Shawn  Bo,  Zcash's  lead   engineer,  explained  that  while  the   project  is  not  quantum  resistant  yet,   most  of  the  protocol  work  for  quantum   resistance  is  already  done.  that  other   channel  can  be  resistant  against  quantum   adversaries  because  it  could  deploy   quantum  resistant  uh  cryptography.   >> Now,  Monero  is  researching  quantum   resistance  as  well,  but  they  are  in   earlier  stages  with  privacy  coins   already  gearing  up.  It's  now  a  question   of  whether  they  can  upgrade  before   quantum  computers  eventually  break   current  cryptography.  So,  what  is  the   verdict?  Are  privacy  coins  just  hype?   Are  they  something  more  durable?  Well,   2025  really  forced  that  question.   Regulators  pushed  harder  and  yet  demand   didn't  disappear.  And  that  of  course   doesn't  prove  privacy  coins  are  the   future.  They're  still  volatile,   vulnerable,  and  dependent  on  how  far   regulation  and  technology  go.  But  it   does  suggest  that  while  part  of  this  may   have  been  driven  by  excitement  or  price,   [music]  it  was  also  possibly  a  reaction   to  the  direction  the  financial  system  is   moving.  Now  whether  that  reaction  grows,   fades,  or  gets  cut  off  entirely  is  still   [music]  an  open  question.  So  where  do   you  think  privacy  coins  will  go  this   year?
summary_hu: 2025-ben a privát coinokat jelentős szabályozói nyomás érte, de ennek ellenére a Zcash és a Monero árfolyama drámaian megugrott. Naval Ravikant tweetje, amely a Bitcoin és a Zcash közötti biztosítást említi, valószínűleg csak szavakat adott ahhoz a már fennálló tendenciához, hogy az emberek egyre inkább vágynak a pénzügyi rendszer átláthatóságán túlmutató lehetőségekre. A privát coinok, különösen a Zcash, amelyet nem érintettek olyan drasztikusan a letiltások, jobban tudtak alkalmazkodni a szabályozási környezethez. Az országok, mint India és Korea, eltérően közelítettek a privát coinokhoz, ami hatással volt a piaci teljesítményre. A videó végén felmerül a kérdés, hogy vajon a privát coinok számára van-e jövőjük, mivel a szabályozás és a technológiai fejlődés folyamatosan alakítja a helyzetet.
summary_en: In 2025, privacy coins faced substantial regulatory pressure, yet both Zcash and Monero experienced dramatic price surges. Naval Ravikant's tweet regarding the insurance aspect of Bitcoin versus Zcash likely articulated an existing trend where individuals increasingly seek alternatives beyond the transparency of the financial system. Privacy coins, especially Zcash which faced fewer delistings, adapted better to regulatory environments. Different approaches from countries like India and South Korea affected market performance differently. The video poses the question of the future viability of privacy coins, given the ongoing influence of regulation and technological advancements.
key_points_hu: A privát coinok árfolyama, különösen a Zcash, jelentős mértékben emelkedett a szabályozói nyomás ellenére.
Naval Ravikant tweetje rávilágított a pénzügyi rendszer átalakulására és az emberek alternatívák iránti igényére.
A különböző országok eltérő szabályozói hozzáállásai befolyásolták a privát coinok piaci teljesítményét.
key_points_en: The prices of privacy coins, particularly Zcash, significantly increased despite regulatory pressure.
Naval Ravikant's tweet highlighted the transformation of the financial system and people's demand for alternatives.
Different regulatory approaches from countries affected the market performance of privacy coins.
